summary: codebuff-cli impersonates Codebuff and drops mutable binaries from an unaffiliated GitHub repo with TLS verification disabled
details: Package is named `codebuff-cli` and its README instructs users to install it as `codebuff`, copying the product description, install instructions, and SDK references verbatim from the legitimate CodebuffAI/codebuff project. However, the bin wrapper at `cli/bin/codebuff.cjs` does not fetch from the CodebuffAI organization. Instead, it queries `https://api.github.com/repos/Marcus-Mok-GH/codebuff-cli/releases/latest` (an unaffiliated personal GitHub account), downloads the platform-specific binary referenced by the mutable `latest` tag to `~/.codebuff/bin/codebuff`, runs `fs.chmodSync(destPath, 0o755)`, and `spawn`s it. There is no commit pin, no version pin, no hash check, and no signature verification — every CLI invocation pulls whatever bytes the personal-account owner currently labels `latest`. Additionally, when spawning the downloaded binary, the wrapper injects `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0` into the child environment, disabling TLS certificate validation for all HTTPS traffic the binary performs (including the AI-assistant backend the README references). A developer who follows the README believing this is the real Codebuff CLI ends up running arbitrary native code from an unaffiliated third party with TLS validation switched off, exposing the source code and prompts uploaded by the assistant to network-position attackers. The combination of impersonation, publisher mismatch, mutable unverified binary fetch, and explicit TLS-disable is unambiguously a supply-chain attack shape, not aggressive-but-legitimate engineering.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
